sentences.text: OK.
I'm Judith Rubenstein, family name Schwarcz.
I am 24-- I am 74 now.
In 1920, September the 19th.
I was born in Hungary.
Called a-- small town called Mezocsat, where my father's family comes from.
Not really.
My father got a post-- position as a teacher in a bigger city, and we moved when I was six years old.
And I lived in that town, called-- named Szerencs.
And we lived there until deportation came.
His name was Elias Schwarcz.
He was-- when we arrived to Auschwitz, he was 54.
He was a teacher of religion.
And we had no parochial school in our town, so every religion had a right to send their representative into the public schools and high schools, to teach their own children their own faith.
So my father represented the Jewish community.
And he was giving two, three times a week-- had a class in various level of the children.
He also was-- and the one who kept the records of the Jewish community-- comings, goings, birth, death.
And he was well-known and well-liked in the community.
We were four children.
He was a very gentle man.
He was a great-- had a great mind.
He was a great scholar.
He was-- every minute of what he could spare, he was studying.
And anything we wanted to know, he had an answer.
Was a very gentle man.
He was-- he was never interested in politic.
He was-- he was a good man.
A good, peace-loving person.
Yes, he was orthodox, but in a modern way.
My life was not restricted absolutely.
I went to school-- in public school, with my friends.
Most of them were Gentile girls.
And we got along beautifully.
I went to the theaters, and movies, books.
He was very free.
He gave me a free hand.
Any kind.
Any kind.
Now, since it was a smaller town, we-- we didn't have much of an opportunity for others.
And I loved reading.
My father instilled into us the love of reading.
And I used the library quite often.
And most of my education came from books, especially after when the restriction came of the Jew-- and Jewish kids couldn't go to school anymore.
So books were the only source of information we could get.
Well, he-- he-- he studied the Talmud.
And anything-- anything.
We had a daily newspaper, the weekly-- weekly Jewish paper, and then magazines-- whatever.
He was always-- book was always on the table.
Hungarian and German, also Yiddish.
But German was his second language.
I studied in school also.
I wasn't too fluent, but I could master.
Her name was Rose.
Rose Hofstetter.
She-- my two grandmothers were friends way back, where they came from.
And this was some kind of a family-- a-- a very loose family connection.
But when my parents got married, there was a very close-knit family on both side.
She was 44.
I remember it very clearly, because her-- I was born on her birthday, actually, one day later.
She was born in 19-- 1900, September the 20th.
So we celebrated our birthday together.
She was a fine woman.
A real homemaker, a good mother, a gentle soul.
She really didn't know much what's going on in the world.
She was involved to raise her family.
And they were very close, with-- my mother came from a family of 11 children.
And they were close, always coming and going.
Uncles, aunts visited us very often from other cities.
So we grew up as cousins, really close.
Peace.
Peace, beautiful.
That was the day when we were all sitting around the table, talking.
My father was telling, and we discussed whatever was a current event.
He knew what's going on in the politic.
And since he had a great mind, whatever we wanted to know.
And he had the patience to listen to us.
So we had a lot of questions to-- to ask him.
What's going on in the world?
What, you know, was beyond us?
Because we didn't believe in a big city.
So we didn't-- and then the latest years, the propaganda was so strong, influenced by the German government, that we really didn't know what's going on-- exactly what's going on.
And my father analyzed the situation.
And unfortunately, he saw-- he foresaw what is coming.
Not so clearly, but he felt that something is bad happening.
I had no sisters.
I had three brothers.
And we were spaced out.
My brother, who is alive-- the only one who is alive-- lives in Israel.
He's three years younger from me.
And when he came that age, my father sent him to the yeshiva for higher learning and Talmudic learning.
He worked-- he studied till he hit 19 years old, and then he went up to Budapest to work.
My next brother--
He's Yitzhak.
Then I had this next brother, who was 16 in 1944.
He was very bright.
I think he was the smartest of all, among us.
He was very bright and very ambitious.
He was also in a yeshiva studying.
And of course, they came to elementary school and high school the same time.
He was 16 when we arrived to Auschwitz, and he was killed a few months later.
My friends saw him return to Auschwitz, and there, on the train with his friends.
And by that time, he was in horrible condition.
So I am sure that that was his last day in the world.
Because when they brought back these youngsters, they were all killed.
They couldn't work anymore.
It was just-- His strength gave out.
And then I had a little brother, six years old.
He was a little boy, Emil.
No, in '44-- right.
He was born in 1938, when-- and Mengele just sent him with my-- my mother, to the left-- left side.
And I never saw them again.
Was Simon.
Was Emil.
Well, of course, the other brother, Yitzhak.
Geza was in Hungarian-- because you have to pick a Hungarian name-- because we were in age, we were the closest.
But the boys left home.
And I-- I wouldn't say that I was too close to them, because they were never home.
Just the little one.
He was almost like my own.
I was 18 years old when he was born.
So we loved him very much.
So it was a beautiful child.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] Tell me about Szerencs, about the community.
The community was a very nice-- I mean, as I think back, we had no enemies.
We-- we lived in peace and quiet.
We had no-- anything like in Poland, the ghetto style.
We lived among different religion, but they were all Hungarians.
And I considered myself to be a good Hungarian.
I went to a school where the mother tongue was Hungarian, and I learned.
I studied history.
And I was very proud to be a Hungarian.
We made homework together with the kids, my schoolmates.
And it was for entertainment-- wasn't much.
Every Thursday night, it was-- and we had a beautiful park in the middle of town, and there was a band playing-- so we used to go to walk with my friends and enjoy the music.
Occasionally, a theater group came to town.
And we had a steady movie theater.
We had to get permission from the principal to go to the movie.
Books, mainly.
And then we got together with friends, and we walked.
We lived in a foot of a big mountain.
And that was the most pleasure-- pleasant experience, to go up in the mountains and pick flowers, and just walk around.
That was the summertime.
We had no-- anything like summer camp.
When we got restless, my father just put us on the train-- when we were older-- and sent us to Grandpa's.
And we spent one summer at one grandparent's home, who lived in another town.
And then by the other grandparents'.
And there we met all the cousins, and we had a great time, just to be together with them.
Our needs-- I mean, we didn't know that we missed anything.
We had a happy-- quite happy, contented life.
My father made a comfortable living, and my mother was a good homemaker, and we were well fed, had good friends.
And that was it.
It wasn't too-- too large, but it was a very close, close-knit.
And we knew everyone.
If there was a wedding, or it was a sad occasion, everybody was involved.
So it was not too large-- I mean, you know.
About 10,000.
It had the biggest chocolate-- sugar chocolate factory, but it belonged to the-- it was government-sponsored.
And that was the biggest sugar factory in Hungary.
So it was quite a large population of workers-- worked.
And among the-- in the technicians and engineers, were a few Jewish men, educated men.
They lived in a compound together there.
But it was a very peaceful existence.
Until-- until Hitler came to power.
In 1938, we started to feel the crunch.
Very polite-- very polite, respectful, everybody-- . you know, we kept the Shabbat.
But my mother was so careful that, it came Sunday, she would never allow us to do anything-- to hang up clothes, or wash, or to insult the neighbors.
Because we lived in the same land.
And we respected when they had Christmas.
And then when we had our Hanukkah, there was-- everybody knew about it.
We used to share with them matzo on Passover.
Give to-- we lived in a rented house-- and I remember my mother used to, in a starched napkin, fold in the matzo and take it as a sign of respect to their landlady.
It was very well received.
And we had-- we didn't have much in common, really.
But it was peace, quiet.
Yes, little by little.
Before the war?
Little by little.
Actually, it started when Hitler came to power.
The real power.
But it came-- we started to feel-- feel it in 1938.
The first Jewish law came out in Hungary.
There was a restriction on-- they called it numerus clausus-- where the high universities were closed for Jewish kids.
There was just a certain amount was allowed.
And then, little, little, little, they closed then, the high schools.
They had a very strict selection.
And then they took every-- they-- these licenses.
My husband had a flour mill, and they took the license away.
So he had to-- he had to give it away, practically, because nobody paid for it.
The government.
The government-- the Hungarian government.
Yeah, it started to be fascist.
And it's-- got-- every day got stronger and stronger.
And a new restriction came that we didn't have before.
So they took from government offices-- all the Jewish guys were let-- let out-- dismissed.
And then came to the schools.
We were still had-- by that time, I was not in elementary school-- but the children had problems.
And the anti-Semitic children, they heard from their parents.
Or mainly the Youth-- everywhere the Youth started.
It was something new, something exciting.
They wore a uniform.
And they felt very important.
The Youth-- the Nazi sympathizers.
The Youth.
And usually, it worked like everywhere-- the scum, the one who had nothing.
And they had-- they felt that they are getting elevated with-- by the joining the Nazi party-- what they called the Nyilas in Hungary-- but it was the same thing.
And instead of all black shirts, they wore a green shirts.
And it was very painful.
For example, we had a woman-- a washer woman-- because everything was done in the house-- we had-- Auntie Julie, who came to our house for years and years to wash the monthly big wash day.
And my mother was very kind to her.
She had no husband and had four children, and had one son.
The son used to come into our house, and my mother always gave her a-- him a big slice of bread with something, and my brothers' outgrown clothes.
And that kid never had a decent haircut, because the mother had had no money.
And he was really a half-orphan.
But it was no problem.
He-- he was almost part of our family.
And when the Nazis started to come to power, this guy-- his name was Gyula-- he became a big shot.
All of the sudden he became a somebody.
And we were scared of him.
Scared-- he went overboard with the hatred.
1930-- after '39, yes.
When the Nazi-- the Nazi-- Hungarian Nazi party officially came into being.
And then they signed up, all these young kids.
Not all, but many of them.
When they had a miserable home, or they had no home life-- something was missing from their lives, so they find that was a kick for them-- it was a big excitement, wearing uniform.
And then they got the gun.
And they were dangerous.
There were big signs all over town-- 'Jews, go to Palestine.' We would love to go, if we could.
But unfortunately, we couldn't, because there was a restriction there, too.
With the White Paper-- and we just couldn't get out.
Yeah, well, we did dear.
Of course we heard.
But--
In a biased-- before the war-- it was all biased.
The Kristallnacht, of course we knew.
And was-- in Austria, an event was the Anschluss, when the-- which Nazis marched into Austria.
And the-- the-- he committed suicide.
What was his name?
What-- can't remember-- it's starting with a D.
I can't recall his name.
And all of the sudden, it's-- it just became hell on earth.
And the Austrians just went on a rampage.
And the--the Jews-- had no mercy for the Jews.
The stores were closed, and they took everyday livelihood.
And that was before our turn.
So we heard about their plight.
And my father, as he was working for the Jewish community, he used to send little parcels of margarine, and reasoned that they should be able to make wine for Passover.
And little packets, by the hundreds, was mailed out from our town.
And in the beginning, we got letters from these unfortunate people, thanking, that they're very grateful for it.
All of the sudden, the parcels started to come back.
That-- unknown-- the address unknown.
There's no-- no return address.
So that was enough to understand that they are not there anymore.
And then my father had an-- an aunt-- my grandfather had a sister-- who lived in Galicia.
And she was a schoolteacher.
And my father corresponded with her all the time.
And then we-- we started to spend her these care packages.
And then there was nothing that came back.
Didn't even come back.
It was just, simply we didn't hear from them anymore.
So we understood that something bad is going on.
And surely was-- the propaganda papers felt justified that the Jews was kicked out.
And they didn't talk about killing-- just they were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] relocated.
That was the word they used-- relocated.
And there was one in-- was in the paper.
Was when a large group of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German citizens, who were originally from Poland-- well, they didn't get their citizen paper.
But their children were born and raised in Germany.
And they couldn't prove that they're a German citizen.
All of the sudden, they kicked them over the border, over to Poland.
Poland didn't want to accept them.
So they were in a no man land for weeks.
It was wintertime, and I still remember.
We were crying.
We felt sorry for them, but we had no way to help them.
The newspaper.
Surely was-- you know, the Hungarian propaganda paper felt justified that these people were the undesired element.
They were not Germans.
They had no right to be there.
But these people had nothing to do with Germany.
And-- I don't know.
Can I tell an interesting incident happened?
In our own town-- it's later, a few years later, happened the same thing.
And one couple, who had two children.
And my father, since he was an educated man, they came to him.
He was writing letters, and trying to-- to the government that-- you know-- to prove that they were born in Hungary.
Well, it didn't help.
Nothing helped.
And they were kicked over to the border.
We never heard of them.
When I was in Auschwitz working, in 1944, one day a man came into the women's camp, fixing some electricity.
And heard us talk Hungarian, so he started to talk to us.
Said, how come you-- you speak Hungarian?
You are not Hungarian.
I said, yes, I am.
I am from Hungary.
I was born there.
Which town?
So he said Szerencs, and I got all excited.
I said, who are you?
So he mentioned his name-- his name was Miki Mangl.
And then I-- I realized that this guy was kicked over the border.
He lived in Poland for a while, and ended up in Auschwitz the same-- in the same place as I was, and my family.
That's right.
Tried to help, but unfortunately, it didn't work out.
Yes, very much so.
Very much so.
They were throwing stones, they-- breaking windows, marching, yelling.
You know, 'kill the Jews!' And it was very frightening.
We were frightened by that time.
So we just-- we were very subdued, where we-- we closed-- I remembered with Passover, we had the double windows in the living room--
My father put pillows between the windows-- the double windows-- that the sound shouldn't get out.
Because the-- the house was close to the road-- I mean to the sidewalk.
And we were afraid.
We were afraid.
But still we were home-- still.
And then my father was called into the labor force in '41.
He was, at that time-- '41, he was in his early 50s.
And they kept him for a while.
He was demolishing some old buildings.
There was not much to do.
And they sent him home.
He was too old.
But the young ones, they called in.
And little by little, hardly any men left in our town-- Jewish men.
They were all sent out to the front and picking mines.
Well, he didn't know.
Just-- it was-- they had the registration in the city hall, and they knew of-- of everyone.
It came in a telegram.
Telegram that he called into a certain unit, has to be in a certain time.
And he had to go.
A certain unit of labor-- battalion, they called it, in different cities.
And from there, they made it into units.
And they sent them out.
My husband was working on the-- by the-- by the border-- Polish-Hungarian border, up in the mountains.
No-- no, they were the leaders.
They became the captains and the lieutenants.
And some were decent.
Most of the them were not.
All of the sudden, it got into their head that they became somebodys.
And to-- to ruin-- to hurt the Jew-- it was-- it was an inhuman treatment in many of these battalions.
It was terrible.
And this went on from 1938 till '44.
And then the Red Cross sent the card, my-- a telegram that is disappeared, is unknown.
And some were lucky.
They got over to the Russian-- captured by the Russians.
But most of them were killed by-- they were between the two, the Russian and German-Hungarian army.
They were everybody's [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] prey.
Well, we-- we were very frightened by that time.
And we tried to live quietly, and do what we had to do.
I was working by that time as a dress maker.
And I had many customers-- Gentile customers.
I had no problem with them.
We didn't talk about politic.
Some of them, I knew that they were Nazi sympathizers.
But that was something else.
I was doing a-- a service for them.
They paid me.
But we didn't discuss politic.
And we really didn't want it to start, because it was very painful.
All of the sudden, these-- my good school friends, whom I really have worked homework together-- all of the sudden, they just turned their head, or walked over to the other side.
It was a very, you know, humiliating.
But had to accept it.
Myself, personally, no.
In Slovakia, they took all the young girls, from the age of 16, I think.
But in Hungary, they didn't.
They called that, for the war effort, if we want to go to work, surely we went to sign up.
But they never took advantage.
There was nothing, really, going on.
The war was not in Hungary.
It was in the border, over in the Polish side.
And they-- they out of the planes they flew over the bombs.
But we didn't-- we-- we had-- you know, we were quite secure at that time.
And then the first bomb fell on Budapest.
That was a provocated-- was provoked by the Germans.
They killed the-- our president's son.
And that was really a provocation.
And they blamed it on the Americans, and they started the war with-- with America.
And the first bomb fell out in Budapest, my husband was injured.
He was working in a labor battalion, demolishing old houses.
And he was buried alive there.
Luckily, he made it.
But a lot of people were killed then.
And after then, it was down.
Sliding down.
Every day, harder and harder.
New laws that you're not supposed to do this, you can do this, food.
And then we started to get a the ticket, a ration ticket for food.
And the-- the Germans took everything out from Hungary.
So the people, in general, suffered-- was a loss of-- with-- life started to be hard.
And was a great inflation, and the stores stayed open.
Opened, but was empty.
And so when it came 1944, it was a very [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nervous city.
Very-- everybody was on-- you know, it just felt that something is coming.
Something unpleasant.
